title: How the inferior frontal gyrus fails to inhibit trauma responses
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
The brain region you have never heard of 
 There is a small piece of your brain tucked behind your left temple. About the size of a walnut. Scientists cal...
htmlContent:
The brain region you have never heard of

There is a small piece of your brain tucked behind your left temple. About the size of a walnut. Scientists call it the inferior frontal gyrus. I call it the part of me that keeps failing when I need it most.

Here is the thing. This region is supposed to help you stop. It is supposed to put the brakes on automatic reactions. When your nervous system screams "danger" and your body prepares to fight, flee, or freeze, the inferior frontal gyrus should step in and say, "Wait. We are safe. We can pause."

But for those of us with complex trauma, that voice is often silent.

I remember sitting in my therapist's office three years ago, describing how I had completely lost my temper at a grocery store clerk who asked me to move my cart. A small thing. A nothing thing. And I was shaking, heart pounding, ready to bolt or bite. She asked me what happened between the clerk's words and my reaction. I stared at her. "Nothing," I said. "There was no gap."

That is the inferior frontal gyrus failing. It is supposed to create a gap. A millisecond of space where you can choose a different response. But trauma erodes that gap until it disappears entirely.

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed polyvagal theory, would say my nervous system was doing exactly what it was trained to do. It was detecting cues of danger that my conscious mind could not even register. The clerk's tone. The way she shifted her weight. Something. Nothing. And my body acted before my brain could catch up.

This is not a character flaw. It is a wiring problem.

What the inferior frontal gyrus actually does

Let me break this down in plain language. The inferior frontal gyrus, or IFG for short, sits in the prefrontal cortex. That is the part of your brain that makes you human. It handles impulse control, emotional regulation, and something called response inhibition. That is a fancy way of saying it stops you from doing the thing your lizard brain wants to do.

Think about it. Every time you resist the urge to scream at a driver who cuts you off, your IFG is working. Every time you take a breath instead of snapping at your partner, your IFG is working. Every time you feel fear rising and choose to stay in the room anyway, your IFG is working.

But trauma changes this. Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, has spent decades showing how trauma gets trapped in the body. What he and others have found is that chronic stress and early adversity actually shrink the prefrontal cortex. They reduce the volume of gray matter in the IFG. They weaken the connections between this braking system and the amygdala, which is your brain's alarm bell.

So here is what happens. The alarm goes off. The amygdala screams FIRE. And the IFG, the fire marshal, is sitting there with a broken radio. It cannot hear the alarm. It cannot send the all-clear signal. It just sits there, useless, while your body floods with cortisol and adrenaline and you react like a cornered animal.

I have a friend who was in a car accident five years ago. Every time she hears a loud bang, she hits the floor. Not a choice. Not a decision. Her body goes down before her brain can process what the sound was. Her IFG is not failing because she is weak. It is failing because trauma rewired it.

Wild, right? The very system that is supposed to protect you becomes the system that betrays you.

Why traditional approaches miss the mark

Here is where I get frustrated with the wellness industry. They tell you to breathe. They tell you to think positive thoughts. They tell you to reframe your experiences. And none of that works when the IFG is not online.

You cannot think your way out of a nervous system that is stuck in survival mode. You cannot affirm your way into a calm state when your brain's braking mechanism is physically compromised.

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote the book that changed everything for so many of us, has shown that trauma is not a story you tell yourself. It is a physical imprint on your body and brain. A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Van der Kolk walks through the neuroscience in a way that makes you feel seen, not pathologized. He explains why talk therapy alone often fails and why body-based approaches are essential.

What he and others have discovered is that the IFG can be strengthened. But not through willpower. Not through forcing yourself to calm down. It happens through bottom-up processing. Through the body. Through movement, breath, and sensory experiences that signal safety to the nervous system.

I have been practicing something called orienting. It is a simple exercise from Somatic Experiencing. You look around the room and notice five things you can see. Four things you can touch. Three things you can hear. Two things you can smell. One thing you can taste. That is it. That is the practice.

And what I have noticed is that this simple act of orienting to the present moment actually wakes up the IFG. It creates that gap. That millisecond of space. It tells the brain, "Look around. We are here. We are not there."

Stay with me here. This is not about fixing yourself overnight. It is about understanding that your brain's wiring can change. Neuroplasticity is real. But it requires repetition. Patience. And a willingness to work with the body, not against it.

The inherited dimension of IFG dysfunction

There is another layer to this that people rarely talk about. The IFG does not just fail because of your own trauma. It can fail because of your parents' trauma. And their parents' trauma. And so on.

If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You (paid link) by Mark Wolynn. He explores how trauma gets passed down through epigenetics, through family narratives, through the very wiring of our brains before we are even born.

I think about my grandmother. She fled a war. She never spoke about it. But her nervous system was locked in survival mode for decades. She passed that on to my mother. And my mother passed it on to me. Not through words. Through the way she held her body. The way she startled at sudden noises. The way she could not tolerate silence.

My IFG did not stand a chance. It was born into a lineage of nervous systems that were trained to react, not respond.

Gabor Mate, the physician who has spent his career studying trauma and addiction, says that trauma is not what happens to you. It is what happens inside you as a result of what happens to you. That internal wounding reshapes the brain. It reshapes the IFG. It reshapes how you move through the world.

But here is the hope. The brain remains plastic throughout life. The IFG can be retrained. It can learn to brake again. It can learn to create that gap.

What actually helps rebuild the IFG

I am not going to give you a five-step plan. I hate those. But I will tell you what has worked for me and what the research supports.

First, slow down. I mean really slow down. Not in a performative wellness way. In a practical, daily way. When you feel the urge to react, try to count to three before you speak. That is it. Three seconds. That tiny pause is your IFG getting a workout. It is like lifting a weight for your brain.

Second, work with your body. Somatic therapy. Yoga that focuses on interoception, which is the ability to feel what is happening inside your body. Dance. Anything that gets you out of your head and into your physical experience. Pat Ogden, a pioneer in sensorimotor psychotherapy, has shown that trauma lives in the body and must be addressed there.

Third, educate yourself. Understanding the neuroscience helps. It reduces shame. It helps you stop blaming yourself for reactions that are not your fault. A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Oprah Winfrey and Bruce Perry. It reframes the question from "what is wrong with you" to "what happened to you." That shift alone can change how you relate to your own nervous system.

Fourth, rest. The IFG is energy-intensive. When you are exhausted, it is the first system to go offline. That is why you snap at people when you are tired. That is why your emotional regulation crumbles after a long day. Rest is not a luxury. It is a neurological necessity.

I know this is hard. I know you want to be fixed yesterday. I know you are tired of reacting in ways that hurt you and the people you love. I see you. I am you.

But here is the truth I am still learning: the IFG is not broken forever. It is just weak. It needs practice. It needs patience. It needs the right kind of input.

And maybe, just maybe, it needs you to stop hating it for failing. It is not your enemy. It is a part of you that got overwhelmed by a world that was not safe. It did the best it could with what it had.

Now you get to give it something better.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link) by Gabor Mate. It will make you angry at how much we have been sold a story that mind and body are separate. They are not. Never were. And the IFG is just one small piece of that whole, beautiful, wounded, healing system that is you.

Healing Journey Resources

  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - Bessel van der Kolk's essential work on trauma and the body
  • What Happened to You? (paid link) - Oprah Winfrey and Bruce Perry's compassionate reframing of trauma
  • It Didn't Start with You (paid link) - Mark Wolynn on inherited family trauma patterns
  • When the Body Says No (paid link) - Gabor Mate on the connection between stress and illness
